metadata.title: Why re-reading your own order back to the user catches errors parsing never will
metadata.topic: Confirmation as a synchronization barrier before irreversible actions
metadata.purpose: Confirmation is a checkpoint where the agent's representation of the action must match the user's actual intent; the user's mind flags a mismatch the parser had no way to see, before the write is committed.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro af_kore) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.exclusions: specific airline rules, payment processing internals
metadata.visual_object: A timeline — hidden user intent → agent's parsed action → recited details → user scans/corrects → execute — with intent and parse drawn diverging, then snapping together at confirmation.
metadata.hook: Before any database write, the agent must list every detail back and get an explicit "yes" — a step that looks redundant until it catches a mistake the parser accepted.

beats.narration_text: The agent recites "Cancel reservation ABC: refund $450 to credit card ••4567, 2 passengers, 5–7 days"; the user stops it — "I wanted a travel certificate, not a card refund" — the agent re-lists corrected details and only then executes.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: The agent already parsed the request correctly by its own lights; why does forcing the user to re-affirm catch errors that would otherwise commit silently?
Before any database write, the agent must list every detail back and get an explicit "yes" — a step that looks redundant until it catches a mistake the parser accepted.

Confirmation is a checkpoint where the agent's representation of the action must match the user's actual intent; the user's mind flags a mismatch the parser had no way to see, before the write is committed.
beats.visual: A timeline — hidden user intent → agent's parsed action → recited details → user scans/corrects → execute — with intent and parse drawn diverging, then snapping together at confirmation.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: User asks for $30 insurance for 2 passengers; agent recites "Insurance: $60 (2 × $30)"; user: "only one passenger" — caught before payment commits.

Confirmation is a checkpoint where the agent's representation of the action must match the user's actual intent; the user's mind flags a mismatch the parser had no way to see, before the write is committed.
